steps.title: Find what the blank needs to supply
steps.content:
The blank fills in the subject and verb of a relative clause that attributes a quoted phrase to a specific critic. The clause needs a common noun title ("critic"), the critic's proper name ("Stina Chyn"), and a verb ("claims") that connects to the integrated quotation. Eliminate any choice that uses punctuation where none is needed.
  • Choice A — 'critic, Stina Chyn, claims': Wraps the name in commas, treating it as non-restrictive (removable) extra information. But the name is essential to identify which critic — commas are wrong. ✗
  • Choice B — 'critic, Stina Chyn, claims,': Same comma error around the name, plus an additional comma before the integrated quotation that no rule requires. Double error. ✗
  • Choice C — 'critic Stina Chyn claims': No commas around the name (correctly restrictive) and no comma before the integrated quotation. Both conventions satisfied. ✓
  • Choice D — 'critic Stina Chyn, claims,': No comma error around the name, but places an unneeded comma between the verb "claims" and its integrated quotation. ✗

steps.title: Confirm with the two conventions at play
steps.content: Two rules govern this blank: (1) a restrictive appositive — one that uniquely identifies the noun — takes no commas; (2) an integrated quotation needs no comma between it and the preceding verb. Only one option satisfies both simultaneously. That matches choice C — C is correct.
